coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode detection of tertiary butylhydroquinone in edible oils based on copper‑cerium oxide nanozyme and nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wanyi
Zhou, Chenyu
Wang, Junshu
Wang, Hanbo
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: To ensure a sensitive and accurate assessment of potential health hazards posed by tertiary butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) in food products, we developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode sensing system for TBHQ monitoring, which integrated copper‑cerium oxide nanocubes (Cu-CeO2 NCs) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). The Cu-CeO2 NCs with excellent peroxidase-like activity could catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP). The DAP exhibited ultraviolet absorption at 415 nm with fluorescence emission at 565 nm, that could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs at 469 nm via inner filter effect. TBHQ with strong antioxidant capacity inhibited the oxidation of OPD, thus restoring the fluorescence of N-CDs. Therefore, a dual-mode sensing platform was established for TBHQ analysis with the limits of detection of 0.64 and 0.81 μg/mL, respectively. This platform demonstrated good practicability through its successful implementation in detecting TBHQ in edible oils.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Tertiary butylhydroquinone
Dual-mode detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TBHQ in real samples
Characterizations of the Cu-CeO2 NCs and N-CDs
Assessment of peroxidase-mimicking activity of Cu-CeO2 NCs
Recovery test of TBHQ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the preparation of (A) Cu-CeO2 NCs and (B) N-CDs. (C) The mechanism diagram of the dual-mode detection system for TBHQ.

(A) TEM image, (B) HRTEM image, and (C) the particle distribution of N-CDs. (D) XPS full spectrum and high-resolution (E) N 1s and (F) C 1s XPS spectra of N-CDs. (G) FT-IR spectrum, (H) XRD patterns, and (I) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs.
(A) TEM image, (B) XRD patterns, (C) XPS spectra of Cu-CeO2 NCs. High-resolution spectra of (D) Ce 3d, (E) Cu 2p, and (F) O 1s.

The absorption spectra of OPD + Cu-CeO2 NCs with or without H2O2 (A). Curves of Michaelis-Menten for Cu-CeO2 NCs with OPD (B) and H2O2 (D) as substrates. (C, E) The Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal diagram. (F) The effect of pH (the black line) and temperature (the red line) on the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CeO2 NCs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD, N-CDs, OPD/H2O2, OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2 NCs, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2 NCs/N-CDs, N-CDs/OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2 NCs/TBHQ. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of OPD, N-CDs, OPD/H2O2, OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2 NCs, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2 NCs/N-CDs, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2 NCs/N-CDs/TBHQ. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs. (D) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs with or without DAP.

Fluorescence spectra of OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs/H2O2/N-CDs sensing platform with different concentrations of TBHQ (A). Linear relationship between F469/F565 and the concentration of TBHQ (B). Absorption spectra of OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs/H2O2/N-CDs system with different concentrations of TBHQ (C). Linear relationship between ∆A and TBHQ concentration (D).
(A, C) Selectivity study of OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs/H2O2/N-CDs system for TBHQ assay. (B, D) Anti-interference capability of OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs/H2O2/N-CDs for TBHQ detection.
The dual-mode analysis of TBHQ in real samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode detection of tertiary butylhydroquinone in edible oils based on copper‑cerium oxide nanozyme and nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To ensure a sensitive and accurate assessment of potential health hazards posed by tertiary butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) in food products, we developed a novel ratiometric fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode sensing system for TBHQ monitoring, which integrated copper‑cerium oxide nanocubes (Cu-CeO2 NCs) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). The Cu-CeO2 NCs with excellent peroxidase-like activity could catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP). The DAP exhibited ultraviolet absorption at 415 nm with fluorescence emission at 565 nm, that could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs at 469 nm via inner filter effect. TBHQ with strong antioxidant capacity inhibited the oxidation of OPD, thus restoring the fluorescence of N-CDs. Therefore, a dual-mode sensing platform was established for TBHQ analysis with the limits of detection of 0.64 and 0.81 μg/mL, respectively. This platform demonstrated good practicability through its successful implementation in detecting TBHQ in edible oils.

The mechanism diagram of dual-mode (ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric) detection for TBHQ based on copper-cerium oxide nanocubes (Cu-CeO2 NCs) and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). TBHQ can be quantified by monitoring the ratio of the fluorescence signals F469/F565 and the UV–vis absorption at 415 nm.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tertiary butylhydroquinone (TBHQ), as a widely utilized oil-soluble antioxidant, is frequently added to food, and its excellent preservative properties can effectively extend the shelf life of food (Peng et al., 2024). TBHQ has strong capabilities in scavenging and neutralizing free radicals, making it a commonly used agent for delaying the oxidative degradation of edible oils. (Liu et al., 2020; Sohaib et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2023). By effectively mitigating degradation and rancidity, it significantly contributes to preserving the integrity and safeguarding the optimal quality of fat-rich foods and industrial products (Mishra et al., 2020; Pu et al., 2018). While TBHQ exhibits desirable characteristics, its use as a food additive continues to be a subject of debate worldwide. According to reports, the permissible daily intake for human is limited to 0.7 mg/kg of body weight, and its maximum allowable addition in food is set at 0.2 g/kg (Liu, Qu, et al., 2024). Consuming excessive amounts of TBHQ can result in severe health issues, including anaphylaxis, impaired immune system, liver damage, and gastric tumor (Balram et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, accurate detection of TBHQ is crucial to maintaining food security and human health (Han et al., 2022). At present, although immunochromatographic assay (Xu et al., 2022), liquid chromatography (Xu et al., 2016), electrochemical analysis (Cao et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2018; Sebastian et al., 2022; Tahernejad-Javazmi et al., 2019), and capillary electrophoresis (Xiang & Gao, 2011) have been widely used in the detection of TBHQ, these methods generally face challenges such as high cost, insufficient sensitivity, dependence on professional operators, and cumbersome equipment. More importantly, the single-mode detection method is characterized by poor stability and errors caused by uncontrollable factors. Therefore, the development of an accurate and reliable assay strategy capable of simultaneous multi-signal outputs for the detection of TBHQ has great significance.

Recently, nanozymes have attracted considerable attention owing to their cost-effectiveness, excellent stability, and excellent catalytic performance (Hou et al., 2023; Meng et al., 2022; Song et al., 2022; Zhang, Feng, et al., 2022). Among them, CeO2 nanozyme is noteworthy for its unique catalytic ability, showing a wide range of catalytic activities including oxidase-like (Li et al., 2021), catalase-like (Qiu et al., 2024), and phosphatase-like (Liu & Liu, 2020). Its key property is that it can exist in two valence states Ce3+ and Ce4+. Through the Ce3+/Ce4+ redox cycling mechanism, it efficiently stores and releases reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Lian et al., 2019). However, the peroxidase-like activity of bare CeO2 nanomaterials does not exhibit superior performance (Yang et al., 2021). Among the various optimization routes, a particularly promising approach involves incorporation or deposition of metal elements to enhance the catalytic performance of CeO2. Pang et al. suggested that Co doping in CeO2 may increase the number of defects and introduce a large number of oxygen vacancies. The Co3O4/CeO2 heterostructure exhibited significant enzyme-like activity was applied for the colorimetric detection of tannic acid (Pang et al., 2024). Li et al. employed a simple in situ growth method to modify Au nanoparticles (NPs) on CeO2. This strategy enhanced the catalytic activity through the strong interaction between Au NPs and CeO2, thus facilitating the rapid detection of uric acid (Li, Li, et al., 2024). Yao et al. synthesized Mn-doped CeO2 nanorods using hydrothermal method, and the Mn doping effectively enhanced the peroxidase-like activity of CeO2 (Yao et al., 2024). Notably, Cu-CeO2 nanoparticles crafted through copper doping have significantly improved the peroxidase mimic activity, offering a more economical and efficient catalytic alternative compared to natural enzymes. (Liu, Zhu, et al., 2024).

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are non-toxic, have excellent biocompatibility, photoluminescence properties, and high photostability (Zhao & Tan, 2021). These excellent properties make them highly suitable for applications in electrocatalysis (Li, Gao, et al., 2024), energy conversion, biological imaging (Liu et al., 2023; Tiwari et al., 2023), and fluorescent probes detection (Wang et al., 2024; DZhang, Han, et al., 2022). Nitrogen stands as the most prevalently employed dopant in carbon dots, due to its comparable size to carbon and versatility in forming a variety of chemical structures (Sahu et al., 2022). The doped carbon dots exhibit versatility, particularly in the realm of ratiometric fluorescence detection, where their exceptional luminescence properties are utilized to devise sophisticated sensing systems.

Herein, the copper‑cerium oxide nanocubes (Cu-CeO2 NCs) with remarkable peroxidase-mimetic activity was synthesized via hydrothermal method. Cu-CeO2 NCs could catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of OPD to DAP with ultraviolet absorption at 415 nm and fluoresces emission at 565 nm. DAP significantly decreased the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 469 nm via inner filter effect (IFE). The addition of TBHQ inhibited the catalytic oxidization of OPD in the presence of Cu-CeO2 NCs and H2O2, resulting in a decrease in UV–vis absorption at 415 nm and fluorescence at 565 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was restored (Fig. 1C). Thus, a novel sensing platform for the quantification of TBHQ was developed by monitoring the ratiometric fluorescence intensity at F469/F565 and UV–vis absorption at 415 nm. The accuracy of TBHQ detection was strengthened through the integration of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric assay.

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), H2O2 (30 %), O-phenylenediamine (OPD), and Polyethylene Polyamine (PEPA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and ammonia solution (25 %) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China) Co., Ltd. All chemicals are analytical grade and used directly without purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water with a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ·cm−1.

The U-5100 UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to obtain ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra. Fluorescence spectra were registered using the F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Kyoto, Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were examined by a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were obtained by Rigaku D/max-2550 diffractometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi unit.

The Cu-CeO2 nanocubes were synthesized following the procedure in the previous literature with slight modifications (Cheng et al., 2021). The specific synthesis method is as following: 0.0180 g of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 0.0650 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were introduced into ultrapure water (15 mL) at room temperature, then 200 μL of 25 % NH3·H2O was added to the mixture. Following a thorough stirring of 10 min, the solution was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Afterwards, the solution was transferred to a Teflon reactor and subjected to heating at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the precipitate was then obtained by centrifuging the solution and then thoroughly washing it three times with ultrapure water (Fig. 1A).

The N-CDs were prepared according to previous report with some modification (Fig. 1B) (Zheng et al., 2014). Typically, 4.2 g of citric acid was added to polyethylene polyamine (20 mL) and sonicated for 15 min. A 50 mL round-bottom flask was used to heat the resultant homogenous mixture to 170 °C and kept at this temperature for 4 h. Subsequently, acetone precipitation is used to obtain the product and dialyze for two days using a 3500 Da bag with ultrapure water changed every 6 h. Finally, a yellow solid product is obtained by freeze-drying the dialysis fluid.

H2O2 (1 mmol/L), OPD (1 mmol/L), 30 μL of Cu-CeO2 NCs (2 mg/mL), and Tris-HCl buffer (600 μL, pH 5.0) were successively introduced into ultrapure water and reacted for 15 min, then added 45 μL of N-CDs (1 mg/mL) to the resultant mixture. The absorption spectrum of the solution was measured.
The concentrations of substrate OPD and H2O2 were varied in order to conduct steady-state kinetic studies. The Michaelis-Menten equations are used to determine the kinetic parameters.

Ratiometric fluorescence assay: Various concentrations of TBHQ (20 μL), Tris-HCl buffer (400 μL, pH 5.0), H2O2 (100 μL, 10 mmol/L), OPD (100 μL, 10 mmol/L), and Cu-CeO2 NCs (20 μL, 2 mg/mL) were sequentially added to 330 μL ultrapure water and allowed to react at 65 °C for 20 min. Subsequently, 30 μL of N-CDs (1 mg/mL) was added, and the fluorescence spectrum of the resultant solution was then measured.

Colorimetric assay: Various concentrations of TBHQ (30 μL), Tris-HCl buffer (600 μL, pH 5.0), H2O2 (150 μL,10 mmol/L) and OPD (150 μL,10 mmol/L), Cu-CeO2 NCs (30 μL, 2 mg/mL) were sequentially added to 495 μL ultrapure water and allowed to react at 65 °C for 20 min. Subsequently, 45 μL of N-CDs (1 mg/mL) was added, and followed by the measurement of its absorption spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TBHQ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of our TBHQ detection method in practical applications, we utilized two kinds of edible oils (canola and soybean oil, the brand of Cang Mang Yao and Jiu San) sourced from a local supermarket, the initial TBHQ content was unlabeled. Pretreatment is required before detecting TBHQ in edible oil samples, sample processing was performed as following: In a 10 mL centrifuge tube, 3 g of oil sample and 3 mL of methanol were combined, vigorously swirled with a vortex shaker for 5 min, and then repeatedly centrifuged at 5000 rpm. The extract was then collected, filtered through an organic phase membrane filter (0.22 μm), and diluted with methanol. The initial analysis of the original oil samples revealed no detectable levels of TBHQ. To verify the practical applicability of our proposed method, we applied the spiking technique to quantify the TBHQ content in the edible oil samples by introducing various amounts of TBHQ into the treated oil samples. The detection process for TBHQ in edible oils followed the same procedure outlined in section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the Cu-CeO2 NCs and N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 2A and C, the N-CDs exhibit monodisperse spherical nanostructures with an approximate diameter of 2.5 nm. Distinct lattice fringes are observable in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images with spacing values of 0.28 nm and 0.32 nm, respectively (Fig. 2B), which are in close proximity to the characteristic interplanar distances of the (020) and (002) of graphitic carbon. To further analyze the chemical bond information and elemental composition of the synthesized N-doped carbon dots, we employed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2D displays that the XPS analysis demonstrated the presence of three elements in the synthesized N-doped carbon dots, corresponding to C (284.5 eV), N (398.5 eV), and O (530.6 eV). The N 1s spectrum obtained through high-resolution XPS analysis exhibits three peaks at 400.4, 399.1, and 398.1 eV (Fig. 2E), corresponding to the graphitic-N/N-H, pyrrole-N, and pyridine-N electron orbitals, respectively (Zhang, Han, et al., 2022). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s reveals three distinct components (Fig. 2F). Specifically, the peak at 284.7 eV represents the sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in C=C/C-C bonds, the peak at 286.6 eV signifies the sp3 carbon atoms involved in C-N/C-O bonds, and the peak at 287.1 eV corresponds to the sp2 carbon atoms in CO bonds (Zheng et al., 2014).

The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs depicted in Fig. 2G exhibits the stretching vibrations characteristic of the chemical bonds. The broad absorption bands observed within the range of 3500–3000 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and -NH2 groups, whereas the absorption bands located at 1650–1580 cm−1 are mainly the absorption of secondary amines NH and CO trans. The spectral band corresponding to the CN stretching vibrations is found within the range of 1420–1400 cm−1. Based on the FT-IR spectroscopy, it can be verified that numerous amino groups are present on the surface of CDs, indicating the successful implementation of the N-doping. The characteristic diffraction peaks of the N-CDs are clearly visible in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 2H). Specifically, two prominent peaks centered at 2θ values of 29.7° and 42.2° are recognized, corresponding to the graphite lattice planes of (002) and (100), respectively.

Fig. S2A displays the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs under various excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 nm to 390 nm. Spectral analysis reveals that the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of N-CDs is independent of the excitation wavelength. Fig. 2I shows that the maximum emission wavelength of the N-CDs is 469 nm under excitation at 360 nm. The inset of Fig. 2I shows that the solution of N-CDs appears colorless and transparent under sunlight and emits blue fluorescence when irradiated by ultraviolet lamp. Fig. S2B shows the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remained stable when pH varied from 3.0 to 6.0. Fig. S2C demonstrates that even after 30 min of continuous Xe lamp exposure, the N-CDs exhibited minimal change in fluorescence intensity, indicating their extraordinary photostability. In extreme conditions up to 300 mmol/L salt, the stable fluorescence of the N-CDs indicates the excellent chemical stability (Fig. S2D). As mentioned above, N-CDs with significant fluorescence properties and good stability were successfully synthesized.

The SEM image of Cu-CeO2 NCs reveals dense and homogeneous microstructures (Fig. S1). As revealed in Fig. 3A, the TEM image demonstrates that the Cu-CeO2 NCs exhibit an irregular and cubic-like morphology. HRTEM analysis provided further insight, clearly identifying lattice fringes spacings of 0.20 nm, 0.31 nm, and 0.25 nm, which correspond to the (022) and (111) planes of CeO2, and the (002) plane of CuO, respectively (Li et al., 2015). XRD can further analyze the structure of materials, as depicted in Fig. 3B, the diffraction peaks of Cu-CeO2 NCs observed at 2θ angles are precisely located at 28.55°, 33.08°, 47.48°, 56.33°, 59.09°, 69.40°, 76.70°, 79.07°, and 88.41° (Liu, Zhu, et al., 2024). These peaks align perfectly with the characteristic pattern of pure CeO2 (JCPDS No.34–0394), and no impurity phases were observed in the sample. Furthermore, the XRD reveals that there is no CuO diffraction peaks present, which may be caused by either the successful incorporation of Cu within the Ce lattice, leading to its displacement, or the formation of exceedingly tiny CuO clusters (Su et al., 2016). This observation demonstrates the uniform dispersion of Cu throughout the Ce matrix and potentially enhancing catalytic performance of the material.

As depicted in Fig. 3C, the XPS full spectrum demonstrates that Ce, Cu, and O are the elemental compositions of Cu-CeO2 NCs. As evident from Fig. 3D, eight different peaks can be fitted within the intricate Ce 3d spectrum. Six prominent binding energy peaks correspond to the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 states of Ce4+, while two weaker peaks signify the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 states of Ce3+. This observation indicated the coexistence of Ce4+ and Ce3+ in Cu-CeO2 NCs, the presence of Ce3+ attributed to charge transfer facilitated by the robust interaction between ceria and copper oxide. Fig. 3E shows that the characteristic peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 are displayed at the respective energy levels of 932.8 eV and 952.6 eV (Utara et al., 2023). Furthermore, the high-resolution O 1s spectra could be decomposed into three distinct peaks located at 532.6 eV, 531.0 eV, and 528.9 eV (Fig. 3F). The peak with the lowest binding energy, marked as (OL), stems from lattice oxygen interacting with metal ions. Meanwhile, the peak labeled (OV) signifies chemisorption of oxygen or the presence of weakly bonded oxygen species. Lastly, the peak exhibiting the highest binding energy, denoted as (OA), corresponds to surface oxygen associated with adsorbed carbamate or water molecules (Yang et al., 2022). The above validation indicates that the Cu-CeO2 NCs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of peroxidase-mimicking activity of Cu-CeO2 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4A, when H2O2 is introduced into the system, Cu-CeO2 NCs catalyze the oxidation of OPD, leading to the production of DAP that prominently displays an absorption peak at 415 nm. To assess the catalytic performance of Cu-CeO2 NCs, we selected OPD as the chromogenic substrate and calculated the Michaelis-Menten equation. As displayed in Fig. 4B-E, the calculated Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) of Cu-CeO2 NCs for the substrate OPD and H2O2 were 0.043 mmol/L and 0.101 mmol/L, and the maximum velocity (Vmax) of 5.65 and 4.11 × 10−8 M/s for OPD and H2O2, respectively. Cu-CeO2 NCs exhibit extremely high catalytic efficiency in catalytic substrate reactions, which is significantly better than the peroxidase-like activity of other reported nanomaterials (Table S1). We investigated the effect of different Ce:Cu ratios on the catalytic performance of the Cu-CeO2 NCs. Fig. S3A shows that the highest catalytic activity was achieved with a Ce:Cu ratio of 2:1. Cu-CeO2 NCs catalyzed the oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2, and the relative standard deviation of the UV–vis absorbance of DAP at 415 nm was 0.64 % (n = 6), indicating the high reproducibility of the catalytic ability of the Cu-CeO2 NCs nanozyme (Fig. S3B). The enzyme activity of Cu-CeO2 NCs exhibited good stability, remaining stable even after 35 days of storage (Fig. S3C). To optimize the experimental conditions, we investigated the effects of reaction temperature, reaction time, pH, concentrations of OPD, H2O2, and Cu-CeO2 NCs on the peroxidase activity of Cu-CeO2 nanozymes. Fig. 4F demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CeO2 NCs/OPD/H2O2 system reached maximum at pH 5.0 and reaction temperature of 65 °C. The fluorescence intensity of Cu-CeO2 NCs/OPD/H2O2 system increased continuously with reaction time, then kept stable after 20 min (Fig. S4A). The fluorescence intensity tended to be stable when the concentration of Cu-CeO2 NCs was more than 0.04 mg/mL (Fig. S4B). Fig. S4C-D show that when the concentrations of H2O2 and OPD increased, the fluorescence intensity also increased, leveling off at 1 mmol/L. Therefore, we chose pH of 5.0, temperature of 65 °C, reaction time of 20 min, Cu-CeO2 NCs concentration of 0.04 mg/mL, OPD and H2O2 concentrations of 1 mmol/L in subsequent experiments.

We investigated the feasibility and sensing mechanism of OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs/H2O2/N-CDs system for TBHQ detection. Fig. 5A-B show the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of OPD, N-CDs, OPD/H2O2, OPD/Cu-CeO2, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2, OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2/N-CDs, and OPD/H2O2/Cu-CeO2/N-CDs/TBHQ. It is evident that the OPD, N-CDs, and OPD/H2O2 systems show no significant UV–vis absorption peak at 415 nm. When Cu-CeO2 NCs are present with OPD, the Cu-CeO2 NCs can catalyze the oxidation of OPD to DAP with a weak peak appeared at 415 nm. However, a significant increase in the UV–vis absorption peak at 415 nm is observed in the coexistence of H2O2 and Cu-CeO2. The addition of N-CDs to the system slightly enhances the absorption at 415 nm, and the absorption peak significantly decreases with the addition of TBHQ (Fig. 5A). Fig. 5B illustrates that under the catalysis of Cu-CeO2 NCs, OPD is oxidized by ROS generated from H2O2 into DAP, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. However, when TBHQ is added, the amount of ROS decreases, which weakens the DAP fluorescence and restores the fluorescence of N-CDs. The above results demonstrate the dual-mode sensing platform for TBHQ detection is feasible. Then the quenching mechanism was explored. We performed an analysis utilizing the Stern-Volmer equation, which is expressed as F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q]. Ksv denotes the Stern-Volmer quenching coefficient, [Q] signifies the concentration of DAP, F and F0 designate the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the presence and absence of DAP. As depicted in Fig. S5, Ksv remains relatively constant across varying temperatures, indicating that neither static nor dynamic quenching mechanisms are present within the system. The overlap between the fluorescence emission peak of N-CDs and the UV–vis absorption peak of DAP (Fig. 5C) provides the prerequisite for either IFE or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs with and without DAP were measured for further explore the quenching mechanism. It can be seen from Fig. 5D that there is almost no change in the fluorescence lifetime of the two systems. Furthermore, no new absorption peaks appeared after adding N-CDs to the system (Fig. 5A), indicating the quenching mechanism is IFE.

For dual-mode sensing of TBHQ, the performance of the sensing platform under optimal experimental conditions was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 6A, with the increase in TBHQ concentration, the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 469 nm gradually increases, whereas the fluorescence signal of DAP at 565 nm decreases. A good linear relationship was observed between TBHQ concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F469/F565) over the concentration range of 1 to 400 μg/mL (Fig. 6B). The linear regression equation was F469/F565 = 0.459 + 0.00314 [TBHQ] (μg/mL, R2 = 0.996), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.64 μg/mL. We also investigated the colorimetric method for the detection of TBHQ. As depicted in Fig. 6C, an inverse correlation was observed between the concentration of TBHQ and absorption intensity at 415 nm. The variation in absorbance values exhibited a good linear correlation with TBHQ concentrations (1–250 μg/mL), the linear regression equation was ∆A = 0.0167 + 0.00178 [TBHQ] (μg/mL, R2 = 0.993), where ∆A = A0 – A, A0 and A is the absorbance of the system without and with TBHQ. The LOD of TBHQ was 0.81 μg/mL (Fig. 6D). In addition, the relative standard deviations of the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric methods for the detection of 50 μg/mL of TBHQ were lower than 3.48 % and 1.14 % (n = 6), respectively, indicating the high reproducibility of our method. Compared with other reported TBHQ detection methods, our colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode assay platform offers a lower detection limit and wider detection range with higher sensitivity than most existing methods, and dual-mode detection reduces the impact of interfering signals, making results more realistic and reliable. In addition, the Cu-CeO2 NCs and N-CDs are simpler to prepare and the experimental method is straightforward, providing a more convenient method for TBHQ detection (Table S2).

The performance of the sensing platform in complex environments was assessed through selectivity and anti-interference analysis to ensure its stability and reliability in practical applications. We conducted a comprehensive evaluation to investigate the potential effects of common metal ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Cl−), biomolecules (glucose, maltose, fructose, sucrose, urea), amino acids (arginine, tyrosine, phenylalanine), and food preservatives (sodium benzoate) on the selectivity and anti-interference capability of our sensing systems. As can be seen in Fig. 7 A, the ∆A of the OPD/Cu-CeO2 NCs/H2O2/N-CDs system only increased in the presence of TBHQ, while other substances had no effect on the absorbance of the system. Notably, the absorption intensity response to TBHQ remained stable even in the presence of potential interfering substances (Fig. 7B). Fig. 7C demonstrates that only the addition of TBHQ could increase the fluorescence intensity ratio F469/F565, while the existence of other interfering substances had negligible effects on the ratio fluorescence signal of TBHQ (Fig. 7D). The above results demonstrate the excellent selectivity and anti-interference capability of this dual-mode assay platform for TBHQ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery test of TBHQ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode sensing system was examined by detecting the concentration of TBHQ in two edible oils (canola and soybean). The spiked recoveries of both samples were investigated, and the corresponding relative standard deviations (RSDs) were calculated. As shown in Table 1 , the spiked recoveries for the two edible oil samples ranged from 92.05 % to 108.07 %, with the RSDs less than 4.21 %, indicating the reliability of this dual-mode sensing strategy for TBHQ analysis in edible oils. A statistical significance test was performed to compare the results obtained by the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric methods. The t-test showed no statistically significant difference between the outcomes of the two methods at the 99 % confidence level. The experimental results obtained indicate that the proposed sensing platform exhibits considerable potential for the quantitative determination of TBHQ in edible oils.

In summary, a highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform was developed based on Cu-CeO2 NCs and N-CDs for TBHQ determination. TBHQ has antioxidant properties and can scavenge free radicals. Its addition inhibits the catalytic oxidation of OPD to produce DAP by Cu-CeO2 NCs, causing a reduction in UV–vis absorption at 415 nm and fluorescence emission at 565 nm, thereby restoring the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Ratiometric fluorescence provides self-correction, while the colorimetric integration further strengthens TBHQ detection accuracy. The sensing platform has been successfully applied for the detection of TBHQ in edible oil samples, which has promising potential in food safety monitoring and analysis.

Wanyi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Chenyu Zhou: Formal analysis. Junshu Wang: Software, Data curation. Hanbo Wang: Data curation. Xingguang Su: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22374058), the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province, China (No. 20220402001GH), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, China (No. SKLEAC202201).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.143517.
